coredata.dc:title: N,Si co-doped GQDs: Facile green preparation and application in visual identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers and selective quantification of catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Ya-Ya
Zhan, Xin
Wu, Yi-Wei
Yu, Xiao-Xiao
coredata.dc:description: A green economical procedure for preparing N,Si co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,Si-GQDs) using waste toners and ethylene diamine was reported, which not only minimizes waste and promotes recycling but also offers an alternative method for producing N,Si-GQDs. At a pH of 8.5, hydroquinone and catechol underwent oxidation in the presence of air, resulting in the formation of diquinones, specifically p-phenyldiquinone and o-phenyldiquinone. Resorcinol, on the other hand, was converted into monoquinone. The interaction between diquinones and N,Si-GQDs caused a linear fluorescence quenching effect when catechol and hydroquinone were present. However, this effect was minimal in the case of resorcinol. Furthermore, the antioxidants glutathione (GSH) and ascorbic acid (AA) were observed to disrupt the redox equilibrium of catechol and o-phenyldiquinone, leading to the activation of fluorescence. Conversely, hydroquinone and p-phenyldiquinone, due to the highly stable and symmetrical structure of p-phenyldiquinone, did not exhibit this fluorescence activation. Based on the described “Off-On” sensor system, it was possible to visually identify dihydroxybenzene isomers and selectively quantify catechol and hydroquinone in environmental samples, as well as GSH and AA in human serum. The method detection limits were 0.93, 1.35, 2.34, and 1.37 μM for catechol, hydroquinone, GSH, and AA, respectively. In conclusion, the presented procedure offers several advantages, including environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and a means of recycling waste toners. It also demonstrates the successful synthesis of N,Si-GQDs, as well as the potential for their application in the “Off-On” sensor system for the detection and quantification of various analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antioxidants
Dihydroxybenzene isomers
N,Si-GQDs
“Off-on” fluorescence sensor
Visual identifying

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: “Off” fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs for probing catechol and hydroquinone
“On” fluorescent of N,Si-GQDs/catechol for sensing antioxidants (AA/GSH)
Preparation of lake water and human serum
Discussion on the synthesis of N,Si-GQDs
Principle for visual identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers and selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs
Optimization for selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs
Optimization for probing catechol and hydroquinone based on “Off” fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs
Effect of reaction time between N,Si-GQDs and catechol/hydroquinone
Optimization for probing antioxidants (AA/GSH) based on “On” fluorescent of N,Si-GQDs/catechol
Selectivity of “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs sensor
Selectivity of “Off” fluorescent sensor of N,Si-GQDs towards catechol/hydroquinone
Selectivity and mechanism of ‘‘On” fluorescent sensors of N,Si-GQDs/(catechol/hydroquinone) towards AA/GSH
Quenching mechanism of ‘‘Off” fluorescent sensor of N,Si-GQDs for catechol/hydroquinone
Analytical performance for selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs
Application as an “Off-On” fluorescent switching sensor of N,Si-GQDs for selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China ( 2022CFD038 ) and Educational Commission of Hubei Province of China ( B2021129 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative characterization of N,Si-GQDs by TEM (a) and XRD (b).
XPS full survey spectrum of N,Si-GQDs (a), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (b), O 1s (c), N 1s (d), Si 2p (e) and infrared characterization of N,Si-GQDs (f).
High-resolution XPS spectrum of Fe 2p in G waste toner (a) and in the as-prepared GQDs (b); VSM of G waste toner (c) and the as-prepared GQDs (d).
Selectivity of 9.6 × 10 −5 g mL−1 N,Si-GQDs towards 200 μM phenolics (a); Elimination the interference from catechol in detecting hydroquinone (b).
Selectivity of N,Si-GQDs/catechol (a) and N,Si-GQDs/hydroquinone (b) towards 200 μM analytes.

Fluorescence spectra of N,Si-GQDs with varying contents of catechol (5.0–180 μM) (a) and hydroquinone (5.0–200 μM) (b), respectively and fluorescence spectra of N,Si-GQDs/catechol with different contents of AA (5.0–200 μM) (c) and GSH (5.0–200 μM) (d), respectively.

The synthesize route for N,Si-GQDs and construction principle for identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers and selective quantification of catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs.
Analytical results of catechol and hydroquinone in water and antioxidant in human serum.

row.entry: Analytical result of AA and GSH in the human.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Detected by N,Si-GQDs or N,Si-GQDs/Catechol sensor system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated value by subtracting the amount of hydroquinone from the total amount of catechol and hydroquinone.

head.ce:title: N,Si co-doped GQDs: Facile green preparation and application in visual identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers and selective quantification of catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi, 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry
Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi, 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green economical procedure for preparing N,Si co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,Si-GQDs) using waste toners and ethylene diamine was reported, which not only minimizes waste and promotes recycling but also offers an alternative method for producing N,Si-GQDs. At a pH of 8.5, hydroquinone and catechol underwent oxidation in the presence of air, resulting in the formation of diquinones, specifically p-phenyldiquinone and o-phenyldiquinone. Resorcinol, on the other hand, was converted into monoquinone. The interaction between diquinones and N,Si-GQDs caused a linear fluorescence quenching effect when catechol and hydroquinone were present. However, this effect was minimal in the case of resorcinol. Furthermore, the antioxidants glutathione (GSH) and ascorbic acid (AA) were observed to disrupt the redox equilibrium of catechol and o-phenyldiquinone, leading to the activation of fluorescence. Conversely, hydroquinone and p-phenyldiquinone, due to the highly stable and symmetrical structure of p-phenyldiquinone, did not exhibit this fluorescence activation. Based on the described “Off-On” sensor system, it was possible to visually identify dihydroxybenzene isomers and selectively quantify catechol and hydroquinone in environmental samples, as well as GSH and AA in human serum. The method detection limits were 0.93, 1.35, 2.34, and 1.37 μM for catechol, hydroquinone, GSH, and AA, respectively. In conclusion, the presented procedure offers several advantages, including environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and a means of recycling waste toners. It also demonstrates the successful synthesis of N,Si-GQDs, as well as the potential for their application in the “Off-On” sensor system for the detection and quantification of various analytes.

A simple, green and economical method of preparing N,Si co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,Si-GQDs) with 19.12 % quantum yield by using waste toners and ethylene diamine was synthesized. Based on N,Si-GQDs, we established a new “Off-On”fluorescence sensor for visual identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers and selective quantification of catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants (AA and GSH).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:sections.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA); aspartic acid (Asp); boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs); bovine serum albumin (BSA); cystine (Cys); double ionic liquid (DCIL); ethylene diamine (EDA); glutathione (GSH); internal filtration effect (IFE); quantum dots (QDs); other abbreviations are listed in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Dihydroxybenzene isomers [1], specifically catechol, resorcinol, and hydroquinone, have been widespread application in fields such as pharmaceuticals, pesticides, and fine chemicals [2]. However, these compounds pose significant challenges [3] due to their high toxicity, resistance to degradation, and potential health hazards through the food chain when present in the environment for prolonged periods. Ascorbic acid (AA), an antioxidant found in the human body, plays a crucial role in alleviating oxidative stress associated with various diseases. It offers cellular protection, aids in wound healing, prevents atherosclerosis, and bolsters the immune system [4]. Additionally, glutathione (GSH), another antioxidant, plays a vital role in maintaining redox balance, and abnormal levels of GSH within organisms have been linked to conditions such as cancer, Alzheimer's disease, and mitochondrial diseases [5]. Therefore, it is of paramount importance to accurately identify dihydroxybenzene isomers and selectively quantify catechol, hydroquinone in environmental samples, as well as measure the levels of antioxidants (AA and GSH) in serum. These analyses are critical for assessing the impact of the ecological environment and enabling precise disease diagnosis.

Currently, several methods have been employed for the detection of dihydroxybenzene isomers and antioxidants (AA or GSH), including HPLC [6], chemiluminescence [7], electrochemical sensors [8], and colorimetric methods [9]. HPLC is commonly used to identify dihydroxybenzene isomers due to their structural and property similarities [6]. However, separating these isomers using HPLC can be challenging and complex due to the minimal differences in their partition coefficients, leading to overlapping chromatographic peaks. Moreover, HPLC methods are often costly and time-consuming. Fluorescent sensors, on the other hand, offer excellent selectivity and can overcome the limitations of HPLC. These sensors are gaining increasing attention from researchers in the field of analytical chemistry [10–12]. For example, a fluorescent sensor based on a double ionic liquid (DCIL) was developed for the analysis of hydroquinone and catechol. Under alkaline conditions, catechol and hydroquinone were oxidized to the corresponding benzoquinone, which interacted with the DCIL, resulting in fluorescence quenching [12]. While most of the reported fluorescent methods for dihydroxybenzene isomers are quantitative in nature, qualitative identification methods are rare.

In recent years, doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, attracting significant interest from researchers [13,14]. These doped GQDs exhibit excellent photo-stability, improved biocompatibility, and lower toxicity compared to other fluorescent materials. Tang et al. successfully developed a method for detecting GSH in human retinal pigment epithelial cells using MoS2 nanosheets and N-GQDs as the basis for their research [13]. Moreover, N-GQDs were utilized as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of aspartic acid and glutamic acid [14]. However, there have been relatively fewer studies investigating the visual identification of catechol, resorcinol, and hydroquinone isomers using doped GQDs.

In this paper, we utilized ethylene diamine (EDA) and waste toner to prepare N,Si co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,Si-GQDs) through a simple and environmentally friendly one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesis of N,Si-GQDs in this study does not require the use of strong oxidizing acids, making it a safe and green approach. By employing N,Si-GQDs as a fluorescent probe, we attempted to establish an “off-on” sensor for selective quantitative analysis of catechol/hydroquinone and AA/GSH, as well as visual identification of hydroquinone, resorcinol, and catechol. The use of waste toner for producing N,Si-GQDs not only offers convenience, environmental friendliness, and cost-effectiveness but also provides an alternative recycling method. One notable advantage of the proposed method is the successful visual identification of isomers such as catechol, resorcinol, and hydroquinone using N,Si-GQDs. Additionally, we were able to exclusively monitor the coexistence of catechol and hydroquinone by adding 200 μM AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The waste toner used in this study was collected from the dust collector in Excellent Color Technology Co., Ltd. This waste toner represents various types of toners available in the market and is generated as a byproduct of the clean production process. Excellent Color Technology Co., Ltd. produces six main types of products, resulting in seven different types of waste toner. Table S1 provides detailed information on the waste toner samples labeled 1#-6# and the composition and source of the initial waste toner. Please refer to the supplementary material for information on other reagents used in the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instruments
ce:section.ce:para: Instruments involved in this work is X-ray powders diffractometer (XRD) (Zeiss Merlin, Germany), X-ray electron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Fisher K-Alpha, America), comprehensive physical property measurement system PPMS-VSM (VSM) (MPMS XL-5, America), and others is in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for making N,Si-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To dissolve 9 mmol of EDA and 0.1 g of waste toner, 5 mL of deionized water was used. The resulting solution was thoroughly mixed and then heated at 200 °C for 4 h in a small autoclave. After the reaction system was cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to isolate any remaining solid particles. A yellow product was obtained and its concentration is 1.1 × 10−2 g mL−1 [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Off” fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs for probing catechol and hydroquinone
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments were carried out using a total volume of 4.0 mL. Catechol or hydroquinone solutions in pH 8.5 Tris-HCl buffer were prepared and incubated with N,Si-GQDs at a concentration of 9.6 × 10−5 g mL−1 (35 μL of 1.1 × 10−2 g mL−1 GQDs in the final volume of 4 mL solution is 9.6 × 10−5 g mL−1). The incubation was performed for 25 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the fluorescent emission signal of all solutions was measured at 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “On” fluorescent of N,Si-GQDs/catechol for sensing antioxidants (AA/GSH)
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments were conducted with a total volume of 4.0 mL. A series of AA or GSH solutions in pH 8.5 Tris-HCl buffer were mixed with N,Si-GQDs at a concentration of 9.6 × 10−5 g mL−1, along with 180 μM of catechol. The mixture was then incubated for 5 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the fluorescent emission signal of all solutions was measured at 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lake water and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed procedure for preparing the real-world sample is in Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Optimization of N,Si-GQDs preparation
The preparation of N,Si-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for preparing N,Si-GQDs is described in Section: Procedure for making N,Si-GQDs and depicted in Fig. S1. The synthesis route is briefly outlined in the figure. After the reaction, a yellow filtrate was collected, which exhibited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light at 320 nm. The possible structure of N,Si-GQDs was also simulated using Chem Sketch software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ethylenediamine volume
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of EDA volume (200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μL) on fluorescence intensity of N,Si-GQDs was investigated. The results are displayed in Fig. S2, and it is obvious that the optimum is 600 μL EDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of reaction temperature (160, 180, 200 °C) and time (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16 h) on quantum yield of N,Si-GQDs are listed in Table S2. As observed, the quantum yield increases with higher reaction temperatures within 8 h of reaction time. However, at 200 °C, the quantum yield reaches its optimum value of 19.12 % when the reaction time is 4 h. Therefore, the optimal conditions for achieving the highest quantum yield are at a temperature of 200 °C for a duration of 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the synthesis of N,Si-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GQDs involves various carbon sources and oxidizing/reducing reagents, is synthesized using a different approach, resulting in the production of different functional GQDs, such as N,Si-GQDs. The traditional method, known as Hummers, follows a “top-down” approach, where strong oxidizing acids are used to break down the carbon source into graphene [16,17]. Alternatively, the “bottom-up” method involves small molecule carbonization to synthesize GQDs [18]. A detailed comparison of some synthesis methods with the current study is presented in Table S3. It is evident that the one-pot hydrothermal method used in this work, which involves the use of waste toner as the precursor material, does not require any strong oxidizing acids. This makes the synthesis process simpler, greener, safer, and faster. Furthermore, this approach provides a new alternative technology for the recycling of waste toner, adding to its significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N,Si-GQDs characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Characterization and confirmation of the structure and properties of N,Si-GQDs were performed using various instruments, including FTIR, XPS, XRD, and simultaneous thermal analysis.

The TEM image in Fig. 1 (a) reveals lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.236 nm, corresponding to the graphite plane (1120) [19]. Additionally, the average diameter of the N,Si-GQDs is determined to be 1.5 ± 0.25 nm. This observation provides further evidence of the graphite-like nature of the material. The XRD profile in Fig. 1(b) shows a diffraction angle (2θ) of 27.351°, which is consistent with the graphite plane (002) [20]. This finding supports the identification of the material as graphite.

Fig. 2 presents the general survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of the N,Si-GQDs. In Fig. 2(a), the XPS spectrum composition is found to be 55.41 % C 1s (285.26 eV), 22.15 % O 1s (532.26 eV) [21], 8.12 % N 1s (400.52 eV), 14.28 % Si 2p (102.39 eV), and 0.04 % Fe 2p (724.31 eV). These results suggest that the as-prepared graphite material may contain doping elements such as N, Si, and a minimal amount of Fe. Fig. 2(b) shows the fitted peaks, including CO (287.35 eV), C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–N (285.02 eV), C–Si (284.14 eV), and graphene-C (283.52 eV) [19,20,22]. In Fig. 2(c), the two peaks can be attributed mainly to C–O–C (532.22 eV) and CO (531.66 eV) [23]. Fig. 2(d) displays three prominent peaks representing the N–C bond or graphitic N (400.07 eV), nitrates (406.9 eV), and N–H (401.56 eV) [15,24]. However, in Fig. 2(e), two peaks are observed at 101.9 eV (organic-Si) and 102.96 eV (Si–C) [25], indicating that Si may substitute some C atoms in the graphene structure. These XPS spectral results collectively demonstrate the co-doping of N and Si in the as-prepared N,Si-GQDs.

Fig. 2(f) exhibits the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. Several characteristic stretching vibration peaks are observed, including O–Si (979 cm−1) [26], C–O–C (1010 cm−1), the aromatic ring of C–H (2960/2920 cm−1), and O–H/N–H (3430 cm−1). Additionally, there is a peak at 1150 cm−1 corresponding to C–C/C–H stretching vibrations in the aromatic ring, which is lower than 1350 cm−1 [27]. This finding further confirms that the synthesized material is GQDs rather than graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) [28]. Moreover, peaks at 704 cm−1 (secondary amine), 1400 cm−1 (C–N), and 1630 cm−1 (CO vibration absorption peak of CO–NH from the aromatic ring skeleton) also indicate the presence of GQDs rather than GOQDs or carbon dots (CDs) [19,29]. These peaks illustrate the existence of functional groups such as COO-, COOH-, OH-, and NH2 −, contributing to the good water solubility of the synthesized N,Si-GQDs.

To further investigate whether the prepared material was co-doped with Fe in addition to N and Si, high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p in waste toner and the as-prepared GQDs from waste toner were examined. The corresponding results are presented in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). It is evident that the characteristic Fe 2p peaks observed in the waste toner disappear in the as-prepared GQDs [30,31]. Additionally, the weak magnetism observed in the waste toner (Fig. 3(c)) also disappears in the as-prepared GQDs (Fig. 3(d)). Based on these findings, it can be concluded that there is no Fe doping in the as-prepared GQDs, and the material is solely co-doped with N and Si, hence referred to as N,Si-GQDs.

Fig. S3 illustrates the zeta potential (Fig. S2(a)) and thermogravimetric analysis of N,Si-GQDs under oxygen conditions (Fig. S2(b)). Considering that graphene is inherently electron-rich, the zeta potential result of N,Si-GQDs (−1.29 mV) in Fig. S2(a) aligns well with this characteristic.

The DSC and DTA results shown in Fig. S2(b) indicate a slight volatilization of water and residual ethylenediamine in N,Si-GQDs below 100 °C. Additionally, the final weight loss at 600 °C is approximately 80 %, which can be attributed to the presence of C, O, and N in N,Si-GQDs. The remaining 20 % residue may be related to iron oxide and silica oxide, which correlates well with the XPS results shown in Fig. 3(a). Considering all of the aforementioned analytical findings, it can be concluded that the material obtained from the waste carbon powder is indeed N,Si-GQDs.

Fig. S4 depicts the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the N,Si-GQDs. Evidently, a sharp peak is observed at 280–300 nm, while broad absorption peaks are observed at 300–325 nm (red line), which correspond to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [32]. Additionally, fluorescence at 390 nm is observed when excited at 320 nm. The N,Si-GQDs solution emits blue fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet light (365 nm) and appears as a light yellow color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle for visual identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers and selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 provides a concise description of the synthesis route for N,Si-GQDs and the working principle behind the visual identification of dihydroxybenzene isomers and the selective quantification of catechol, hydroquinone, and antioxidants based on the “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs. Under alkaline conditions (pH 8.5), both catechol and hydroquinone undergo oxidation to form diquinones in the presence of air, while the production of resorcinol involves the formation of a monoquinone [12,33]. Due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) between diquinones and N,Si-GQDs, the presence of catechol and hydroquinone results in a linear decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-GQDs, whereas resorcinol exhibits minimal effects on fluorescence. Moreover, in the presence of antioxidants such as AA and GSH, the redox equilibrium between catechol and o-phenyldiquinone was disrupted, leading to the activation of fluorescence in N,Si-GQDs [10]. However, hydroquinone and p-phenyldiquinone are unable to initiate this activation due to the stable symmetrical structure of p-phenyldiquinone. By combining the observed color changes of catechol, hydroquinone, and resorcinol in the presence or absence of N,Si-GQDs, along with their fluorescence variations under 365 nm excitation in the presence of AA, it becomes possible to visually identify the three isomers of dihydroxybenzene. Furthermore, this “Off/On” sensor system enables the successful selective quantification of catechol and hydroquinone in environmental samples, as well as antioxidants (GSH/AA) in serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs
Optimization for probing catechol and hydroquinone based on “Off” fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of N,Si-GQDs concentrations.

In order to achieve optimal sensitivity, different concentrations of N,Si-GQDs (ranging from 0.04 to 0.26 mg mL−1) were tested while keeping the concentrations of catechol or hydroquinone constant at 180 μM in pH 8.5 Tris-HCl buffer solution. As depicted in Fig. S5, the impact of N,Si-GQDs concentration on the quenching effect is minimal. The maximum quenching rate of 73.3 % is observed at a concentration of 9.6 × 10−5 g mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time between N,Si-GQDs and catechol/hydroquinone
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the effect of reaction time (ranging from 0 to 120 min) between N,Si-GQDs and catechol/hydroquinone on the quench efficiency was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, both the quench efficiencies for catechol and hydroquinone increase rapidly within the initial 20 min of reaction time. They then reach their respective maximum values of 81.0 % for catechol and 68.1 % for hydroquinone at 25 and 21 min, respectively. Subsequently, a stable platform is formed as the reaction time is further extended. Consequently, a reaction time of 25 min was determined as the optimal choice for both catechol and hydroquinone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for probing antioxidants (AA/GSH) based on “On” fluorescent of N,Si-GQDs/catechol
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of catechol concentration.

In order to achieve the maximum quenching efficiency, the effect of catechol concentration on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was examined. As shown in Fig. S7, the quench efficiency of N,Si-GQDs at a concentration of 9.6 × 10−5 g mL−1 and an excitation wavelength of 390 nm gradually increases with increasing catechol concentration from 0 to 180 M. The maximum quenching efficiency of 87 % is observed at a catechol concentration of 180 μM.
Effect of reaction time between N,Si-GQDs/catechol and antioxidant (AA/GSH)

Under the optimized conditions, the effects of reaction time (ranging from 0 to 35 min) between N,Si-GQDs/catechol and AA/GSH on the fluorescence activation efficiency of N,Si-GQDs were investigated. The results presented in Fig. S8 show that the fluorescence activation efficiencies of both systems undergo minimal variation and reach a maximum plateau of 54.3 % and 53.8 % at 5 min, illustrating a rapid redox process between catechol and AA/GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs sensor
Selectivity of “Off” fluorescent sensor of N,Si-GQDs towards catechol/hydroquinone
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the feasibility of N,Si-GQDs as a sensor, the fluorescence response towards phenolics was examined. A concentration of 9.6 × 10 −5 g mL−1 N,Si-GQDs was incubated with 200 μM phenolics (including tyrosine, phenol, catechol, resorcinol, and hydroquinone) for 25 min in pH 8.5 Tris-HCl buffer. The results depicted in Fig. 4 (a) demonstrate that there are minimal fluorescence responses observed for tyrosine, phenol, and resorcinol, while significant responses are observed for catechol and hydroquinone. This outcome suggests that N,Si-GQDs exhibit excellent selectivity towards catechol and hydroquinone, respectively, among the tested phenolics.

In order to exclusively monitor catechol and hydroquinone in the presence of each other, an approach was employed to break down the redox equilibrium between catechol and o-phenyldiquinone. Since both catechol and hydroquinone can cause fluorescence quenching in N,Si-GQDs, the goal was to eliminate the interference from catechol when detecting the content of hydroquinone in actual samples. To achieve this, a sufficient amount of AA (200 μM) was added to the system. Although this addition partially restores the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-GQDs, it helps to stabilize the overall fluorescence intensity of the system, effectively eliminating the interference from catechol. A subsequent experiment was conducted to verify this approach, and the results, as shown in Fig. 4(b), support its effectiveness. In this experiment, while fixing the concentrations of N,Si-GQDs and hydroquinone at 9.6 × 10 −5 g mL−1 and 200 μM, respectively, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases upon the addition of catechol at concentrations of 20, 40, and 100 μM (Fig. 4(b): 3, 4, 5). However, with the subsequent addition of 200 μM AA, the fluorescence intensity stabilizes at nearly the same level (Fig. 4(b): 6, 7, 8), confirming that interference from catechol can be effectively eliminated when detecting hydroquinone in actual samples. Therefore, the content of catechol can be determined by subtracting the amount of hydroquinone from the total amount of catechol and hydroquinone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and mechanism of ‘‘On” fluorescent sensors of N,Si-GQDs/(catechol/hydroquinone) towards AA/GSH
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the detection specificity and detection mechanism of N,Si-GQDs/(catechol/hydroquinone) sensors, we investigated the selectivity of the N,Si-GQDs/catechol and N,Si-GQDs/hydroquinone systems towards biomolecules and common ions, respectively. Under optimum conditions, a concentration of 9.6 × 10−5 g mL−1 of N,Si-GQDs was incubated with 200 μM of various common ions (Ac−, NO2 −, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2-, HPO4 −, Cl−, Br− and F−) and 200 μM of different biological molecules (glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), bovine serum albumin (BSA), cystine (Cys), H2S, GSH and AA). The incubation period was 5 min for all samples. Subsequently, the fluorescent emission signal of the solutions was measured at 390 nm, and the results are presented in Fig. 5 . A distinct difference in the response was observed between the N,Si-GQDs/catechol and N,Si-GQDs/hydroquinone systems. Only AA and GSH were able to activate the fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs/catechol, while the other biomolecules and common ions displayed minimal fluorescence response to both the N,Si-GQDs/catechol and N,Si-GQDs/hydroquinone systems. This indicates that the N,Si-GQDs/catechol sensor exhibits excellent selectivity towards antioxidants (AA/GSH). Furthermore, by taking advantage of the difference in fluorescence activation between N,Si-GQDs/catechol and N,Si-GQDs/hydroquinone, we were able to visually identify catechol and hydroquinone. We propose a possible mechanism for this selectivity. In the presence of antioxidants such as AA or GSH, the redox equilibrium of catechol and o-phenyldiquinone was disrupted, leading to fluorescence activation of N,Si-GQDs [10]. However, hydroquinone and p-phenyldiquinone do not cause fluorescence activation due to the strong stable symmetrical structure of p-phenyldiquinone. The slight differences in fluorescence lifetimes observed among N,Si-GQDs/catechol (τ = 5.091 ns), N,Si-GQDs/catechol with AA (τ = 5.032 ns), and N,Si-GQDs/catechol with GSH (τ = 5.006 ns) suggest that a static interaction type plays a dominant role between N,Si-GQDs/catechol and AA/GSH (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of ‘‘Off” fluorescent sensor of N,Si-GQDs for catechol/hydroquinone
ce:section.ce:para: According to the study mentioned in Ref. [12], catechol and hydroquinone readily undergo oxidation under alkaline conditions in the presence of air, leading to the formation of o-benzoquinone and p-benzoquinone as products. Therefore, the absorption spectra of catechol, hydroquinone, o-benzoquinone, and p-benzoquinone were measured and compared with the excitation and emission spectra of N,Si-GQDs (Fig. S10 (a) and (d)). It is evident that both o-benzoquinone and p-benzoquinone exhibit broad absorption in the range of 280–550 nm, which overlaps well with the excitation and emission spectra of N,Si-GQDs (λex = 320 nm, λem = 390 nm). As a result, the fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs was quenched by the presence of o-benzoquinone and p-benzoquinone due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) between these quinones and N,Si-GQDs [12].

To further investigate the quenching mechanism between catechol/hydroquinone and N,Si-GQDs, the fluorescence lifetimes were determined in the presence and absence of catechol/hydroquinone. The results shown in Fig. S10 (b) and (e) indicate that there is no significant change in the fluorescence lifetimes for catechol (τ 0 = 5.089, τ 1 = 5.091 ns) and hydroquinone (τ 0 = 5.089, τ 1 = 4.975 ns). This suggests that static quenching plays a dominant role in the interaction between N,Si-GQDs and catechol/hydroquinone.

Furthermore, the effects of catechol/hydroquinone on the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-GQDs at different temperatures (293 K, 303 K, 313 K) were investigated using the Stern-Volmer equation (F 0 /F = 1+K SV), where F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-GQDs without and with catechol/hydroquinone, respectively, and K SV is the quenching constant. It was observed that there is a strong linear relationship between F 0 /F and the concentration of catechol/hydroquinone, and K SV gradually decreases with increasing temperature from 293 K to 303 K–313 K (Fig. S10 (c) and (f), Table S4). These results further confirm that the dominant quenching mechanism between N,Si-GQDs and catechol/hydroquinone is static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants based on “Off/On” fluorescent switching of N,Si-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, the sensitivity for detecting catechol and hydroquinone was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence of N,Si-GQDs incubated with various concentrations of catechol (5.0–180 μM) and hydroquinone (5.0–200 μM) for 25 min. As shown in Fig. 6 , there is a good linear relationship between F 0 /F and the concentration of both catechol (5.0–40 μM and 40–180 μM) and hydroquinone (5.0–40 μM and 40–200 μM). The analytical performance data for this experiment are listed in Table S5. The detection limits for catechol and hydroquinone are 0.93 μM and 1.35 μM, respectively. When compared to other reported methods listed in Table S6, except for works [34,35], the sensitivity for detecting catechol and hydroquinone using the N,Si-GQDs fluorescence probe is better [36–39] and comparable to reference [12].

Similarly, the fluorescent behavior of N,Si-GQDs/catechol system for detecting antioxidants (AA/GSH) was investigated. Fig. 6 (c) and (d) demonstrate a good linear relationship between F/F 0 and the concentrations of AA (5–100 μM and 100–200 μM) and GSH (5–200 μM), respectively. Table S5 provides the analytical performance data for this experiment. The limits of detection (LOD) for AA and GSH using this method are 1.37 μM and 2.34 μM, respectively. A comparison of the analytical performance of this work with various other methods for detecting AA/GSH is presented in Table S7. While the LODs are lower than some studies [40,41], comparable to others [42–44], and higher than the works [45–47]. This demonstrates that this method exhibits acceptable sensitivity and holds promising potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as an “Off-On” fluorescent switching sensor of N,Si-GQDs for selective monitoring catechol, hydroquinone and antioxidants
ce:section.ce:para: Selective detection of catechol, hydroquinone in lake water, and antioxidants in human serum was conducted under optimized conditions to assess the adaptability of this method. The obtained results, as shown in Table 1 , indicate that the recoveries for catechol, hydroquinone, AA, and GSH fall within the acceptable range of 91.9 %–106.5 %. These satisfactory results demonstrate that the “Off-On” fluorescent switching sensors have excellent potential for selectively detecting catechol/hydroquinone in lake water and AA/GSH in human serum.

To verify the accuracy of the developed sensors, High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) was employed using the chromatographic conditions described in references [48,49] to analyze catechol/hydroquinone in lake water and AA/GSH in human serum. The results are presented in Fig. S11 and Table S8. None of the four analytes were detected by the HPLC technique in the real samples, which makes it difficult to directly compare the accuracy of this work with the established methods for the analysis of catechol/hydroquinone and AA/GSH. Furthermore, based on the data provided in Tables 1, it can be observed that the contents of AA/GSH in human serum could be detected by the established sensor, while HPLC failed to detect them. This result indicates that the sensing method developed in this work is more sensitive than HPLC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, this study demonstrates a simple, green, safe, and efficient method for preparing N,Si-GQDs using waste toner as the precursor material, without the need for strong oxidation acids. This approach offers a new alternative technology for recycling waste toner. Additionally, the combination of different color changes of catechol, hydroquinone, and resorcinol with/without N,Si-GQDs, along with the fluorescence variations in the presence of AA under 365 nm excitation, enabled the visual identification of the three isomers of dihydroxybenzene. Compared to HPLC, this method provides a simpler, more cost-effective, and convenient approach for identifying dihydroxybenzene isomers. Moreover, by harnessing the properties of N,Si-GQDs and the intrinsic fluorescence enhancement between diquinones and N,Si-GQDs, we successfully developed an “Off-On” sensor for the selective quantification of catechol, hydroquinone in environmental water, and antioxidants (AA and GSH) in human serum. This sensor holds great potential for practical application in environmental and biomedical analysis, offering advantages such as selectivity, sensitivity, and ease of use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Credit author statement
ce:section.ce:para: Ya-Ya Lei: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xin Zhan: Investigation, Preparation, Writing – original draft. Yi-Wei Wu and Xiao-Xiao Yu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts to declare. They have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China (2022CFD038) and Educational Commission of Hubei Province of China (B2021129).
